sentences.text: In 1939 there were still the Jewish theater called Judischer Kulturbund.
And they had a lot of activities.
They had plays, they had operettas, they had concerts.
And all the Jewish artists who could not work any place else now came to the Jewish-- Judischer Kulturbund, and there were many, many beautiful events.
And it so happened that my uncle, my Uncle Alfred, the youngest brother of my father, was a musician.
And he played the English horn in the orchestra.
And the Kulturbund wanted to perform great-- the which is an operetta by Kalman.
And children were needed.
There is a scene with children.
And so his little daughter Lucy, who was four at the time, and myself-- I was already 11-- were-- went to the auditions.
And we were both hired, so to speak, for our performances.
But it was a beautiful time.
It was a group of children, and we learned how to dance.
And we, I mean, we were very musical anyway, but we learned how to sing, how to act on stage, we got beautiful costumes.
And it was a great time for us.
Again, within our community-- the outside was horrible for us.
But when we were among ourselves, we did the best.
And my father and his brother Paul-- Paul, they were singing, and they performed, I believe, Handel's oratorio-- an oratorio of Handel.
And they were singing there in the choir.
And other members of the family were also involved in the cultural activities and-- within that setting.
Very scarce.
Yes.
No.
No, no, no, no, we were not-- my-- my-- my father was a very strict man, very correct.
He wouldn't buy anything on the black market.
No.
The little food we got-- and my grandparents-- my grandparents and my aunt, my mother's sister, helped us always with food.
Sometimes it was so funny when my mother said, my god, we have nothing to eat tonight.
And there would come my grandma with something as if she felt-- though she had something to share with us.
And she would bring it, and we had something to eat.
They got much more.
And you see, my grandmother could go in the street where my grandfather had those barber shop all his life long.
He knew-- she knew all the stores.
She would just go and say, I need something for-- for Gertrude, meaning my mother, you know, the girl that grew up there.
And they would give her-- the vegetable lady or the milkman, whoever, they would give a piece of-- a loaf of bread or a cabbage or something, you know.
They would give it to her for us.
Yes, all the time, all the time.
I-- I-- I went to my grandparents every week.

And many times, see, when I was still living in the outskirts and went to the school in the inner city, the school hours once a week were arranged that we also had lessons in the afternoon because the school was so overcrowded.
Our classes were so big.
And so we had to have lessons in the afternoon.
And we had to break in between.
And I would go to my grandparents'-- walk to my grandparents' place.
And my grandma would feed me, and then I would walk back to school.
She would give me 10-- 10 pennies to take the subway, but I walk.
And I saved the money and gave it to my mother.
Yes, Hebrew, Hebrew, and religious Bible history, and Bible studies, sure, and Pentateuch and everything.
It was a very good Jewish education.
Excellent.
That was outside the school.
Never mentioned.
It started to be mentioned in October, '41 when the first transports were leaving Berlin, you know, when the first children were leaving.
Then we started.
Well, we helped as much as we could.
I mean, I remember in October of '41, a girl in my class, Brigita Glaser, was the first one to leave.
And she said, it's October, and it's going to be cold.
And we are going to Poland.
And I have not even a sweater.
We were all very poor.
We had nothing.
I mean, Jews couldn't buy clothing for years, you know.
And I had one sweater.
But this particular sweater I got from the Jewish social help because we had no other way of getting any.
But I gave that sweater to her.
That's what I wanted to bring out-- how we helped each other and [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We were very sad kids actually.
You know, we were very sad children.
I don't remember running around or laughing or making jokes or young teenage girls being giggly or something.
Mm-mm.
That-- that-- that didn't exist.
I don't know that.
April-- September 19.
Yes.
October, the first transports.
No.
No social events.
We-- we met in-- in our apartment.
And I had a whole group of-- of kids from my school-- my class and higher classes.
And we met each Saturday in an apartment.
We rotated.
And the other kids came and joined us.
And that's how I met my husband, my later husband.
He was also brought into that group, and I met him in 1940, in the winter of 1940.
He attended another school.
But at that time he was already out-- out of the school.
He was already an apprentice.
He wanted to-- to become a cook in the-- in the Jewish-- in a Jewish old age home.
He knew-- he knew the mother of one of the boys was also in the kitchen.
And so it came then, she said, why don't you go there too and meet the other kids, you know.
No, no, no.
The-- the Kulturbund closed after the performance I mentioned in '39.
That was it.
That was the end of it.
It went until the mid and August of '39.
That was the end.
No more.
Were in homes but [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my father would still go to synagogue until 1942.
There was one particular temple.
It was an orthodox temple.
And since his brother had died, and he wanted to go in the morning and at night to say Kaddish.
And it was still possible.
And they still gathered in that one synagogue.
But they were always afraid that the Gestapo would come and just take them all.
And then one day they stopped, you know.
The men didn't meet anymore.
Right.
Well, in school was in October and November was the first two girls left.
But at home, it was much worse because the Deutsch family were to leave already in December of '41.
And they just got their papers.
They had to be ready on a certain day and a certain time, and that's it.
And then the Gestapo comes and picks you up.
And that's it, you know.
And that was one of the worst experiences in my young life that this lovely family, these good people, who had not done anything wrong to anybody, were carted off.
The sons, strong young men-- they always sports conscious.
They belonged to the Maccabi Sports Club in earlier years.
They were taken away for nothing.
And that broke my heart.
My young heart was broken to pieces on that day.
I cried for a week, (CRYING) and I couldn't eat.
I was 14 years old.
It was very hard.
It was a horrible time.
No.
They were just told that they will be resettled.
And they packed warm clothes.
And-- and they bought shovels.
And-- and-- and they thought they would farm somewhere out in Poland, you know.
They would get a piece of land.
And-- and-- and work the land and live there.
Yeah.
No.
Nobody survived.
And that-- I found out later that that transport went to Riga, and they were shot on arrival.
So they-- they didn't suffer much.
But they were killed right then and there.
Until June-- June 30, 1942-- all Jewish schools were closed.
And that was also, of course, a very sad event.
But-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Eighth grade.
That's all the schooling I have.
Yeah, that's all the schooling I have-- eighth grade.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the last day of school, of course, our teachers talked to us and wished us well.
And my-- my-- I wrote an article about all of that.
And in the last hour, he played Schubert's 'Unfinished Symphony' for us, and that's how we left.
Of course, with some of the girls in my class and boys, I still stayed in contact after the closing of school as long as they were there.
I volunteered right away to work in the Jewish orphanage again, in the Auerbach Waisenhaus.
We had to do something, you know.
We couldn't sit around doing nothing.
And I-- I worked there in the kitchen until all the children were deported.
I hate the word 'deported,' you know-- just taken away to be murdered.
And that was at the end of August of '42.
I-- I-- I then was at home for a while.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] actually for a pretty long time.
And I-- in the meantime, we had moved to another Jew house.
And those Jewish people there, they worked so hard in the factories day and night.
And I was cooking for everybody.
I did the shopping.
I cooked for everybody.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and-- and this-- this Jewish employment office must have forgotten me at one point, you know.
I didn't get any mail that I should report, so I didn't.
You know, I stayed home and helped where I could help my people.
Yes, we were forced because the Jews in that house first where the Deutsches lived was now cleared of Jews.
There were only one mixed marriage left and my parents and I.
So we had to move out.
This house now became all Aryan, you know.
And we moved into another house where Jews lived, but very close by, just practically around the corner.
Fewer and fewer Jew houses.
At the end, we were the only Jews in that house.
Yeah.
By February 27, 1943.
There was a big action that the Gestapo-- it was a Saturday-- that the Gestapo arrested all the Jews in their place of work.
All the factories wherever the Jews were were raided.
And all the Jews were off, put on trucks, taken to the railroad station, and off they went.
And here, I want to mention something in connection with that day, February 27.
It was a Saturday, as I said.
And my father did not go to work.
He worked at home.
He had to do some figuring out.
What do I know?
But he was home, and the doorbell rang.

And his boss-- know that this man was a member of the Nazi party-- came to our apartment and said, this morning at 6 o'clock the Gestapo called me and told me that I have to have all the Jews in all the places where they work ready.
They are going to come and arrest.
I have been to all the places where my Jews worked, and I chased them all away.
And now, you leave your apartment so the Gestapo can't get you.
And we left.
See, that was awesome.
You have to mention the people who had a heart, you know.
Ja, Otto Lebrecht.
Sure, Otto Lebrecht.
And-- and so we left the apartment with him.
He helped us pack a few things together.
And he took us to my grandparents.
That was the first thing where to go, you know.
And we stayed with my grandparents for a whole week.
And then we had learned slowly but surely that the men and the children from these mixed marriages were released again.
They were not shipped off.
And might-- I have to go back a few weeks before that.
My father was arrested by the Gestapo.
And he was, I think, about a week in-- in their prison or whatever you want to call it and-- to check out the Aryan papers of my mother.
He had to prove that his wife was Aryan.
And then they released him.
OK, so on that February 27, we went to my grandparents', and we stayed there for a week.
And as I said, we heard that Jews from mixed marriages were released.
And after a week we went back home into our own apartment, and nothing was disturbed.
And-- I should mention also that we had to have a star with the word 'Jude' on it on our front door.
And on the apartment door, also, you had to have that.
But anyway, nothing was disturbed.
And we came home.
And we were there for about an hour, and the bell rang.
The Gestapo came to arrest Rosalita Lehmann-- me.
So they arrested me.
They took me with them.
And I was for four days also in that same Gestapo prison, a converted old age home, and I had to prove that my mother is a Aryan.
And after I had done this with all the papers I had, my mother gave me, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was released.
But, I mean, I hear my mother still screaming when they took me away.
I mean, I was 15 years old.
And these guys take her daughter away, you know.
But I came back.
We were just not treated at all.
We were just on the floor in hallways, you know.
It was overcrowded.
I don't remember getting food or anything.

My mother had given me a piece of bread to take along, which I didn't eat, because when I was finally released on one of the lower floors in the old age home, there was behind a gate that was closed off was my girlfriend of mine calling me, calling my name.
She saw me coming down the stairs.
And-- and she-- she said, do you have anything to eat?
And I said, yes, I do.

And I gave her that piece of bread my mother had given me because she was shipped off to Auschwitz, as I learned from that book now that was published in Berlin about all the Jews from Berlin, how they ended up.
Anyway-- and I was released.
And I went back home.
Now, was this the case in all of Germany that the husbands and--
Only in Berlin.
Only in the city of Berlin.
There were about 2,000 mixed marriages.
And on that February 27 when all the Jews were arrested, many of these so-called Aryan women made a protest march on the street in front of the Gestapo prison.
Whether that has any influence of the release of these men or not, I don't know.
But there were quite a lot of these women came from higher-up German families, educated people.
And there were too many. 2,000 is a lot and would have-- they didn't want to know-- the Nazis didn't want to know the population that they were killing off the Jews.
They didn't want to know that.
Anybody-- you know, it was all-- all done at night, of course.
I have to mention that.
And the Gestapo came to pick you up at night, not-- never during the day.
At night.
Anita.
My cousin Anita was the daughter of my father's second brother, Paul, and his wife, Liesel.
As I mentioned before, Paul died in 1942 from sheer exhaustion.
And his wife died in '43.
She was sick.
She contracted tuberculosis-- horrible circumstances all in all.
But they had that little girl, Anita.
And when my aunt died, we took Anita to us.
She was--
No.
No.
She was there.
She was with us at my grandparents' house when we were hiding.
She came to us early in February of '43 before this big factory action.
And when the Gestapo came to pick me up, they asked, are there any other Jews in the apartment?
But since it was night and Anita was only four years old, she was already in bed in another room.
But her little shoes were standing in the room where the Gestapo was.
And my mother was dying inside that they would see these little shoes, but they didn't.
So we said, no, there are no other Jews.
So at that point, Anita was safe because if she would have been arrested at that point, she would have not survived.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And Anita stayed with us, of course, until June, '43.
In June, '43, they arrested everybody who was just left over.
And since she was a Jewish child from both parents, you know-- she had no Aryan mother to protect her.
And my mother's influence wasn't-- it wasn't her daughter, they came and picked that little girl up.
And she was brought to a Gestapo prison which was next to the Jewish hospital in Berlin.
And the Jewish hospital, I have to mention, functioned through all the war until the last day.
Until liberation, we had Jewish doctors.
We had Jewish nurses.
And we had the Jewish hospital.
I wouldn't have gotten sick.
I would go to the Jewish hospital.
Of course, it was all under the Gestapo.
But that was kept-- the Jewish hospital.
So she was brought to that prison there next to the Jewish hospital.

And every time they put a transport together to ship off these children-- she was in a place where they gathered only the children together-- when they put the transport together to ship off the children, she was sick.
And she was in the hospital and not in the prison.
And that's how she was saved.
And later, after the war, I learned that one of the Jewish doctors gave these children injections the minute they heard that a transport was put together, and the children developed fever.
Nothing else.
Just fever.
And that's how the children were sick.
They didn't deport sick children.
No, when you were sick, you were not deported.
No, you had to be well to be deported.
That's correct.
On May 13, I mean, we were liberated in-- in early May.
But we were not able to get to the city until May 13 of 1945, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and we brought her back home.
We brought her back home.
Yeah.
Yes.
Yes, this apartment was occupied by three families-- actually four families.
Very tight, very crowded.
There was that Kessler family, and there was the Greenberg family, and there was a Cohen family.
Greenbergs were two, Cohens were two, Kesslers were three, and Lehmanns were three.
So we were 10 people in that apartment.
And people coming from very different ways, of walks of life, I wanted to say.
But we got along.
We helped each other.
As I said, I went shopping, I cooked, I-- I-- I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that lasted until December, '42.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then they were all taken away.
And then we were alone in that apartment.
And then another mixed marriage was put into the apartment with us, and that's where we were liberated.
My grandmother-- yeah, that's also I have to mention.
My grandmother was living in that old age home.
In June of 1942, they started to deport the old people from the old age homes.
And at that point, my mother said, uh-uh.
We take-- Grandma has to live with us again.
Let me see what I can do for Grandma.
So Grandma moved back with us in '42, in June-- end of June, '42.
I shared my room with her.
And my mother went to the Gestapo, and-- because she got papers.
She got that-- no, not at that point.
So she stayed with us.
In early August of '42-- so after six weeks or so-- she got her papers to be deported.
And my mother went to the Gestapo.
She went to the headquarters to say that she needs-- her mother-in-law has to help her.
She is a sickly-- my mother was already a sickly woman-- and she needs her.
And-- and-- and of course, it was in vain.
And how they treated my mother was incredible.
They threw her out more or less, you know.
OK.
